title: IUGR (Intrauterine Growth Restriction)
oneLiner: Foetus with estimated weight <10th centile for gestational age; increases risk of stillbirth, neonatal morbidity, and requires surveillance to time safe delivery.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Placental abruption:** sudden severe abdo pain, vaginal bleeding (may be concealed), uterine hypertonus, abnormal CTG (late decelerations/bradycardia). **Key finding:** uterine tenderness + fetal distress – immediate senior review, consider emergency CS.
- **Pre-eclampsia with severe features / HELLP syndrome:** IUGR is often due to placental insufficiency from pre-eclampsia – check BP, urinalysis, and sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (if available). **Key finding:** BP ≥160/110 mmHg + headache/epigastric pain or laboratory evidence of haemolysis/elevated LFTs/low platelets. Call anaesthetics and consultant obstetrician.
- **Stillbirth (unrecognised):** absent fetal heart tones on auscultation or on ultrasound. **Key finding:** no fetal movements reported by mother + no heart rate on CTG. Needs senior obstetrician immediately; manage with counselling, induction of labour.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fundal height < expected for gestation on serial measurements (e.g., small-for-dates on symphysis-fundal height chart)
  - Reduced fetal movements (may be noted by mother)
  - Often asymptomatic – picked up on routine ultrasound (estimated fetal weight <10th centile)
  - May present with maternal hypertension/oedema (if secondary to pre-eclampsia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Fundal height ≥2-3 cm below expected (use customised growth chart)
  - Palpation: small, possibly firm uterus
  - Measure maternal BP, check urinalysis for protein
  - Auscultate fetal heart (handheld Doppler – not reliable for pattern; need CTG)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** True IUGR (often due to placental insufficiency, maternal vascular disease, smoking, pre-eclampsia)
- **Constitutional small baby:** (healthy small infant, normal amniotic fluid volume, normal umbilical artery Doppler, normal growth velocity)
- **Less likely:**
  - Genetic syndrome / congenital anomaly (may have structural anomalies on ultrasound, polyhydramnios or oligohydramnios, abnormal anatomy scan)
  - Infection (CMV, toxoplasma, rubella – look for maternal serology, fetal ultrasound features like echogenic bowel, ventriculomegaly)
- **Splitter:** Umbilical artery Doppler waveform – if absent or reversed end-diastolic flow (AREDF) = pathological IUGR; if normal and amniotic fluid normal = likely constitutional small baby.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - CTG for baseline fetal heart rate, variability, accelerations, decelerations
  - Urinalysis (proteinuria → pre-eclampsia)
  - Blood pressure measurement
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, urate (pre-eclampsia screen)
  - sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (if available and <35 weeks – helps rule out pre-eclampsia if low)
  - Coagulation screen if abnormal LFTs/platelets
  - Kleihauer test if reduced fetal movements / recent trauma
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Detailed ultrasound for estimated fetal weight, amniotic fluid index (AFI), umbilical artery Doppler (pulsatility index, presence of end-diastolic flow)
  - **Second-line:** Middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler (cerebral redistribution suggests fetal compromise), ductus venosus Doppler (late sign of acidosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Serial growth scans (every 2-4 weeks depending on severity)
  - Fetal movement kick charts (if patient reliable)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE: EFW <10th centile for gestational age (customised or population chart)
  - Severe IUGR: EFW <3rd centile or <10th centile with abnormal Doppler or oligohydramnios
- **Never-miss test:** Umbilical artery Doppler – omission may miss placental insufficiency and delay delivery, increasing stillbirth risk.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If reduced fetal movements: CTG for 20 minutes. If abnormal (late decelerations, minimal variability, sustained bradycardia) → senior obstetrician review, prepare for emergency delivery.
  - If maternal BP ≥160/110: refer to pre-eclampsia protocol (labetalol IV, magnesium sulphate for seizures, senior input).
  - If confirmed IUGR with abnormal Doppler/oligohydramnios >37 weeks: discuss induction of labour or elective CS.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit for CTG monitoring and steroid course if <34+6 weeks (betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart)
  - Daily CTG + maternal well-being (BP, urinalysis)
  - Plan for delivery: timing depends on gestation and severity.
    - <37 weeks: individualised – deliver if maternal complications, abnormal fetal surveillance (AREDF, ductus venosus a-wave reversal)
    - ≥37 weeks: consider induction or CS if EFW <3rd centile or abnormal Dopplers
  - Ensure magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if delivering <30 weeks (if local policy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Serial growth scans (every 2-4 weeks) if placental function stable
  - Fetal movement awareness
  - Low-dose aspirin if high risk of pre-eclampsia (start by 16 weeks) – but not for isolated IUGR.
  - Lifestyle advice: stop smoking, optimise nutrition, monitor BP at home.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil by mouth if planned for emergency CS; otherwise oral intake normal.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH if prolonged bed rest or pre-eclampsia (avoid if platelets <50 or bleeding).
  - Diet: normal
  - Observation frequency: maternal BP 4-hourly, CTG twice daily or more if concerns, daily urinalysis. Fetal movement chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Betamethasone (Celestone Chronodose) 12mg IM two doses 24 hours apart (for lung maturity if <35 weeks and delivery planned within 7 days)
  - Labetalol 200mg oral stat (if BP >160/100) then 200mg TDS, titrate; IV labetalol 20mg slow IV then 40-80mg repeated if needed [check local policy for escalation doses]
  - Magnesium sulphate 4g IV bolus then 1g/hour infusion for seizure prophylaxis (if severe pre-eclampsia)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Nifedipine modified release 20mg BD (if labetalol contraindicated: asthma)
  - Methyldopa 250mg TDS (first-line in chronic hypertension in pregnancy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Betamethasone: avoid if active infection (contraindicated? – actually can still be given but with caution; discuss with consultant)
  - Labetalol: avoid in asthma, severe bradycardia, heart block
  - Magnesium sulphate: caution in renal impairment; monitor reflexes and respiratory rate
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Magnesium sulphate: reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g., in pre-eclampsia with AKI); [check local policy]
  - Labetalol: caution in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Betamethasone: giving only one dose and not the second – reduces efficacy; clear documentation.
  - Magnesium sulphate: forgetting to check serum magnesium levels before repeat bolus (risk of toxicity) – but usually only used in acute setting.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Stillbirth (most feared)
  - Fetal distress during labour → emergency CS
  - Preterm delivery (iatrogenic) – neonatal ICU admission
  - Maternal: pre-eclampsia, placental abruption, postpartum haemorrhage (due to small placenta/abruption)
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurodevelopmental delay (especially if severe IUGR with growth <3rd centile)
  - Metabolic syndrome in later life (theoretical risk, not managed on acute ward)
  - Growth restriction may persist into childhood
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality and morbidity depend on gestation at delivery, severity of Doppler abnormalities, and presence of pre-eclampsia. With appropriate surveillance and timely delivery (typically by 37 weeks if severe), outcomes are good. Perinatal mortality is increased ~5-fold in IUGR compared to normal growth.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always check umbilical artery Doppler – it’s the single best predictor of fetal compromise.
- If <34 weeks and suspect delivery within 7 days, give corticosteroids promptly – do not delay for full maturation.
- Discuss timing of delivery with senior obstetrician; use CTG and Dopplers to guide – do not delivery solely on EFW <10th centile if all other surveillance normal.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not rely on fundal height alone – ultrasound estimate is needed for diagnosis.
- Do not automatically induce labour at term for all IUGR – if Doppler and CTG are normal and AFI normal, can continue to 40 weeks with serial scans (NICE). But if <3rd centile or abnormal Doppler, deliver by 37 weeks.
- Do not ignore reduced fetal movements in a known IUGR – needs urgent CTG review.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Decreased fetal movements (e.g., less than 10 movements in 2 hours after 28 weeks)
  - New onset severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain (pre-eclampsia)
  - Vaginal bleeding or sudden abdominal pain (abruption)
  - Rupture of membranes or regular contractions
- **Follow-up:**
  - Obstetric day unit or antenatal clinic within 1 week for repeat growth scan and CTG. Earlier if symptoms.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / registrar if:**
  - CTG pathological (late decelerations, recurrent variable decelerations, baseline bradycardia <110 or tachycardia >160, minimal variability)
  - Umbilical artery Doppler shows absent or reversed end-diastolic flow (AREDF) – especially if <34 weeks
  - Maternal BP ≥160/110 or signs of pre-eclampsia (platelets <100, transaminitis, creatinine >90)
  - Any suspicion of placental abruption (vaginal bleed, uterine hypertonus, fetal distress)
  - Maternal NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red parameter)
- **Call ICU / crash team:** Maternal eclamptic fit, cardiac arrest, massive haemorrhage.

rawMarkdown:
## IUGR (Intrauterine Growth Restriction)
**One-liner:** Foetus with estimated weight <10th centile for gestational age; increases risk of stillbirth, neonatal morbidity, and requires surveillance to time safe delivery.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Placental abruption:** sudden severe abdo pain, vaginal bleeding (may be concealed), uterine hypertonus, abnormal CTG (late decelerations/bradycardia). **Key finding:** uterine tenderness + fetal distress – immediate senior review, consider emergency CS.
- **Pre-eclampsia with severe features / HELLP syndrome:** IUGR is often due to placental insufficiency from pre-eclampsia – check BP, urinalysis, and sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (if available). **Key finding:** BP ≥160/110 mmHg + headache/epigastric pain or laboratory evidence of haemolysis/elevated LFTs/low platelets. Call anaesthetics and consultant obstetrician.
- **Stillbirth (unrecognised):** absent fetal heart tones on auscultation or on ultrasound. **Key finding:** no fetal movements reported by mother + no heart rate on CTG. Needs senior obstetrician immediately; manage with counselling, induction of labour.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fundal height < expected for gestation on serial measurements (e.g., small-for-dates on symphysis-fundal height chart)
  - Reduced fetal movements (may be noted by mother)
  - Often asymptomatic – picked up on routine ultrasound (estimated fetal weight <10th centile)
  - May present with maternal hypertension/oedema (if secondary to pre-eclampsia)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Fundal height ≥2-3 cm below expected (use customised growth chart)
  - Palpation: small, possibly firm uterus
  - Measure maternal BP, check urinalysis for protein
  - Auscultate fetal heart (handheld Doppler – not reliable for pattern; need CTG)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** True IUGR (often due to placental insufficiency, maternal vascular disease, smoking, pre-eclampsia)
- **Constitutional small baby:** (healthy small infant, normal amniotic fluid volume, normal umbilical artery Doppler, normal growth velocity)
- **Less likely:**
  - Genetic syndrome / congenital anomaly (may have structural anomalies on ultrasound, polyhydramnios or oligohydramnios, abnormal anatomy scan)
  - Infection (CMV, toxoplasma, rubella – look for maternal serology, fetal ultrasound features like echogenic bowel, ventriculomegaly)
- **Splitter:** Umbilical artery Doppler waveform – if absent or reversed end-diastolic flow (AREDF) = pathological IUGR; if normal and amniotic fluid normal = likely constitutional small baby.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - CTG for baseline fetal heart rate, variability, accelerations, decelerations
  - Urinalysis (proteinuria → pre-eclampsia)
  - Blood pressure measurement
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, urate (pre-eclampsia screen)
  - sFlt-1/PlGF ratio (if available and <35 weeks – helps rule out pre-eclampsia if low)
  - Coagulation screen if abnormal LFTs/platelets
  - Kleihauer test if reduced fetal movements / recent trauma
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Detailed ultrasound for estimated fetal weight, amniotic fluid index (AFI), umbilical artery Doppler (pulsatility index, presence of end-diastolic flow)
  - **Second-line:** Middle cerebral artery (MCA) Doppler (cerebral redistribution suggests fetal compromise), ductus venosus Doppler (late sign of acidosis)
- **Special tests:**
  - Serial growth scans (every 2-4 weeks depending on severity)
  - Fetal movement kick charts (if patient reliable)
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - NICE: EFW <10th centile for gestational age (customised or population chart)
  - Severe IUGR: EFW <3rd centile or <10th centile with abnormal Doppler or oligohydramnios
- **Never-miss test:** Umbilical artery Doppler – omission may miss placental insufficiency and delay delivery, increasing stillbirth risk.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If reduced fetal movements: CTG for 20 minutes. If abnormal (late decelerations, minimal variability, sustained bradycardia) → senior obstetrician review, prepare for emergency delivery.
  - If maternal BP ≥160/110: refer to pre-eclampsia protocol (labetalol IV, magnesium sulphate for seizures, senior input).
  - If confirmed IUGR with abnormal Doppler/oligohydramnios >37 weeks: discuss induction of labour or elective CS.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit for CTG monitoring and steroid course if <34+6 weeks (betamethasone 12mg IM x2 doses 24h apart)
  - Daily CTG + maternal well-being (BP, urinalysis)
  - Plan for delivery: timing depends on gestation and severity.
    - <37 weeks: individualised – deliver if maternal complications, abnormal fetal surveillance (AREDF, ductus venosus a-wave reversal)
    - ≥37 weeks: consider induction or CS if EFW <3rd centile or abnormal Dopplers
  - Ensure magnesium sulphate for neuroprotection if delivering <30 weeks (if local policy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Serial growth scans (every 2-4 weeks) if placental function stable
  - Fetal movement awareness
  - Low-dose aspirin if high risk of pre-eclampsia (start by 16 weeks) – but not for isolated IUGR.
  - Lifestyle advice: stop smoking, optimise nutrition, monitor BP at home.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: nil by mouth if planned for emergency CS; otherwise oral intake normal.
  - VTE prophylaxis: consider LMWH if prolonged bed rest or pre-eclampsia (avoid if platelets <50 or bleeding).
  - Diet: normal
  - Observation frequency: maternal BP 4-hourly, CTG twice daily or more if concerns, daily urinalysis. Fetal movement chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Betamethasone (Celestone Chronodose) 12mg IM two doses 24 hours apart (for lung maturity if <35 weeks and delivery planned within 7 days)
  - Labetalol 200mg oral stat (if BP >160/100) then 200mg TDS, titrate; IV labetalol 20mg slow IV then 40-80mg repeated if needed [check local policy for escalation doses]
  - Magnesium sulphate 4g IV bolus then 1g/hour infusion for seizure prophylaxis (if severe pre-eclampsia)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Nifedipine modified release 20mg BD (if labetalol contraindicated: asthma)
  - Methyldopa 250mg TDS (first-line in chronic hypertension in pregnancy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Betamethasone: avoid if active infection (contraindicated? – actually can still be given but with caution; discuss with consultant)
  - Labetalol: avoid in asthma, severe bradycardia, heart block
  - Magnesium sulphate: caution in renal impairment; monitor reflexes and respiratory rate
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Magnesium sulphate: reduce dose in renal impairment (e.g., in pre-eclampsia with AKI); [check local policy]
  - Labetalol: caution in hepatic impairment
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - Betamethasone: giving only one dose and not the second – reduces efficacy; clear documentation.
  - Magnesium sulphate: forgetting to check serum magnesium levels before repeat bolus (risk of toxicity) – but usually only used in acute setting.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Stillbirth (most feared)
  - Fetal distress during labour → emergency CS
  - Preterm delivery (iatrogenic) – neonatal ICU admission
  - Maternal: pre-eclampsia, placental abruption, postpartum haemorrhage (due to small placenta/abruption)
- **Chronic:**
  - Neurodevelopmental delay (especially if severe IUGR with growth <3rd centile)
  - Metabolic syndrome in later life (theoretical risk, not managed on acute ward)
  - Growth restriction may persist into childhood

### Prognosis
- Mortality and morbidity depend on gestation at delivery, severity of Doppler abnormalities, and presence of pre-eclampsia. With appropriate surveillance and timely delivery (typically by 37 weeks if severe), outcomes are good. Perinatal mortality is increased ~5-fold in IUGR compared to normal growth.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always check umbilical artery Doppler – it’s the single best predictor of fetal compromise.
- If <34 weeks and suspect delivery within 7 days, give corticosteroids promptly – do not delay for full maturation.
- Discuss timing of delivery with senior obstetrician; use CTG and Dopplers to guide – do not delivery solely on EFW <10th centile if all other surveillance normal.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not rely on fundal height alone – ultrasound estimate is needed for diagnosis.
- Do not automatically induce labour at term for all IUGR – if Doppler and CTG are normal and AFI normal, can continue to 40 weeks with serial scans (NICE). But if <3rd centile or abnormal Doppler, deliver by 37 weeks.
- Do not ignore reduced fetal movements in a known IUGR – needs urgent CTG review.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Decreased fetal movements (e.g., less than 10 movements in 2 hours after 28 weeks)
  - New onset severe headache, visual disturbance, epigastric pain (pre-eclampsia)
  - Vaginal bleeding or sudden abdominal pain (abruption)
  - Rupture of membranes or regular contractions
- **Follow-up:**
  - Obstetric day unit or antenatal clinic within 1 week for repeat growth scan and CTG. Earlier if symptoms.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / registrar if:**
  - CTG pathological (late decelerations, recurrent variable decelerations, baseline bradycardia <110 or tachycardia >160, minimal variability)
  - Umbilical artery Doppler shows absent or reversed end-diastolic flow (AREDF) – especially if <34 weeks
  - Maternal BP ≥160/110 or signs of pre-eclampsia (platelets <100, transaminitis, creatinine >90)
  - Any suspicion of placental abruption (vaginal bleed, uterine hypertonus, fetal distress)
  - Maternal NEWS2 ≥5 (or any single red parameter)
- **Call ICU / crash team:** Maternal eclamptic fit, cardiac arrest, massive haemorrhage.
